evals.name: title-formulas-and-anti-patterns
evals.prompt: Generate 10 title candidates for a 2500-word case study about how a backend team cut Postgres latency 80% by removing one index. The case study is a retrospective with specific numbers (P99 latency 480ms→120ms, took 2 weeks to investigate, the index that was removed served queries that ran <100/day) and a 'what we'd do differently' section. Save the candidates as a numbered Markdown list (1. through 10.) to titles.md in the current working directory. Do not include any other prose — just the list of 10 candidates. Don't ask follow-up questions.
evals.expected_output: A titles.md with exactly 10 distinct title candidates, none of which use anti-pattern phrases (Ultimate Guide, Mastering X, Everything You Need, X is Dead, etc.), most landing in the 8-12 word band, and representing at least 3 distinct title-formula structures.
expectations: titles.md contains exactly 10 numbered title candidates AND none of them contains any of these anti-pattern phrases (case-insensitive substring match): 'ultimate guide', 'everything you need', 'complete guide', 'complete tutorial', 'mastering', 'is dead', 'will change everything', 'will revolutionize', '101 guide', '101:', 'beyond ', 'the truth about', 'shocking', 'secret of', 'one weird trick', 'mind-blow', 'game-chang'. The skill teaches these as anti-patterns; baseline reaches for at least one of them across 10 candidates by default. Failing condition: even ONE candidate contains any banned phrase.

AT LEAST 6 of the 10 candidates have a word count in the 8-12 word range (inclusive on both ends; words separated by whitespace, hyphenated compounds count as one word, numbers count as words). Candidates of 1-7 words are too vague; candidates of 13+ words read as subtitles. Baseline tends to produce a mix skewed toward shorter (4-7 word) titles or longer SEO-padded ones. The skill explicitly teaches the 8-12 word band.

The 10 candidates collectively represent AT LEAST 3 DISTINCT title-formula shapes from the skill's named library — identifiable by these structural patterns: (a) HOW-WE — starts with 'How we' or 'How [team/company] '; (b) WHY-X-IS — starts with 'Why' AND contains a value verb (is, doesn't, won't, can't); (c) THE-X-THAT — starts with 'The' and is followed by a noun phrase ending with 'that' + verb clause; (d) N-LESSONS — starts with a digit/numeral followed by 'lessons', 'things', 'mistakes', 'rules', 'truths' — or 'What [N] [time/events] taught'; (e) ARTIFACT-FORMS — title is mostly a specific number paired with a specific result (e.g. 'P99 latency 480→120ms: a Postgres case study'). The 10 candidates must collectively cover at least 3 of {a, b, c, d, e}; using only 1-2 patterns repeatedly does NOT satisfy this.

evals.name: dek-meta-og-discipline
evals.prompt: I have an article titled 'Why VACUUM blocks during long transactions in Postgres'. Write the full distribution package: (1) a dek/subtitle of 25-50 words; (2) a meta description of 150-160 characters for SEO; (3) an Open Graph image concept described in one paragraph (what should the image show — be concrete). Save all three to package.md with clear Markdown headings (## Dek, ## Meta description, ## OG image). Don't ask follow-up questions.
evals.expected_output: A package.md whose dek extends the title rather than repeating it, whose meta description is in the 150-160 char band with concrete previewed deliverables, and whose OG image concept describes a specific non-stock-photo visual.
expectations: The Dek section in package.md does NOT repeat the title — measured by: NO consecutive substring of 5+ words from the title appears in the dek. Specifically, the dek must NOT contain the exact phrase 'VACUUM blocks during long transactions' or 'Why VACUUM blocks' anywhere in the dek body. The skill explicitly teaches 'dek extends, doesn't repeat'; baseline often opens the dek by restating the title. Lowercased substring comparison; punctuation differences ignored.

The Meta description section in package.md has a character count between 140 and 165 characters inclusive (giving slight tolerance around the 150-160 band) AND contains AT LEAST 2 mechanism words connecting concrete deliverables — mechanism words: 'how', 'why', 'what', 'and', 'plus', 'including', 'because', 'so', '+'. Examples that pass: 'How dead tuples accumulate, why VACUUM stalls, and the alert that catches it'. Examples that fail: 'Learn about Postgres VACUUM' (too short, no mechanism), generic 200+ char marketing prose. Baseline tends to either overflow or write 60-100 char generic descriptions.

The OG image section in package.md describes a SPECIFIC non-generic visual concept. The description must NOT consist solely of any of these baseline-default placeholder ideas (case-insensitive): 'stock photo', 'a developer at a computer', 'a database server', 'the company logo', 'a generic image', 'a person typing', 'an abstract design'. The description MUST mention AT LEAST ONE of: rendered typography of the title, a specific chart/diagram concept tied to the article (e.g. timeline, transaction-state graph, before/after metric), a code/terminal-output visualization, or a specific contextual photograph (with subject named — not 'a developer' but 'a postgres terminal output' or 'a chart of dead-tuple growth over time'). The skill teaches that OG images extend the article's promise; generic stock images are explicitly named as anti-patterns.

evals.name: channel-reframing-three-ways
evals.prompt: I just published an article titled 'How we cut Postgres latency 80% by removing one index' (a 2500-word case study about a 2-week investigation that found one rarely-used index was triggering query planner pessimization on a hot path; the team removed it, P99 dropped 480ms→120ms). Write three distinct launch posts for the article: (1) X/Twitter post (≤280 characters); (2) LinkedIn post (100-300 words); (3) Hacker News submission title PLUS a first comment from the author (the comment should add context that the article doesn't fully cover). Save all three to launch.md with clear Markdown headings (## X, ## LinkedIn, ## Hacker News). Don't ask follow-up questions.
evals.expected_output: A launch.md whose three posts are structurally distinct (not minor variants), where the LinkedIn post uses first-person and avoids LinkedIn-cliches, the HN first comment adds substantive context without promotional language, and X leads with value not the link.
expectations: The three sections in launch.md are structurally distinct, NOT minor variants of the same prose. Specifically: (a) the X/Twitter section's first 60 characters must contain a specific number (digits) OR a contrarian reversal word ('but', 'actually', 'turns out', 'surprisingly', 'not'); (b) the LinkedIn section's first sentence must contain at least one first-person pronoun ('I', 'we', 'our', 'my') OR a specific scene/moment marker (a date, time, or named team event); (c) the Hacker News section must contain BOTH a submission title AND a first-comment subsection. ALL THREE must hold. The skill teaches per-channel framing; baseline tends to copy the same intro across all three.

The LinkedIn section is between 100 and 320 words inclusive AND uses at least 3 paragraph breaks (i.e. at least 4 paragraphs separated by blank lines) AND does NOT contain any of these LinkedIn-cliche phrases (case-insensitive substring match): 'excited to share', 'thrilled to announce', 'honored to', 'humbled to', 'proud to', 'thoughts?', 'what do you think?', 'would love to hear', 'let me know in the comments', 'agree or disagree', 'drop a 🙌'. Baseline LinkedIn posts reach for at least one of these by default; the skill explicitly names them as anti-patterns.

The Hacker News first-comment section adds substantive non-promotional context AND does NOT contain any of these promotional phrases (case-insensitive): 'excited to share', 'we built', 'check it out', 'learn more', 'would love your feedback', 'happy to answer questions about [our product]', 'shameless plug'. The comment must include at least ONE of: a reference to a specific Postgres version or query-planner behavior, a related-work reference (citing another tool/paper/post by name), an explicit acknowledged limitation of the approach, OR an invitation to disagree on a specific named technical point. Generic 'happy to discuss' without a specific point does NOT count. The skill teaches HN first-comments must be substantive context, not promotion.
